---
title: "Deeper-Dive Analysis Framework: Non-College Graduates Without Meaningful Workforce Participation"
editor: visual
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(ggrepel)
library(scales)
library(shiny)
library(shinycssloaders)
library(ggiraph)
library(kableExtra)
library(rmapshaper)
library(cowplot)
library(DT)
library(htmlwidgets)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(lubridate)
library(systemfonts)
reset_font_cache()
library(ggtext)
library(gmodels)
library(fastDummies)
library(car)
library(glmnet)
library(glmnetUtils)
library(pscl)
library(sjPlot)
library(ggforce)
library(openxlsx)
```

```{r themes and shapefiles, include=FALSE}
theme_bar <- theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth  = 0.1),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = "transparent"),
        legend.key = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

theme_line <- theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = "transparent"),
        legend.key = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.1),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))


theme_sf <- theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "white"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

regions <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Join docs/county_regions.csv") %>%
    select(5,6) %>%
    unique() %>%
    mutate(edr = str_replace(edr, "  ", " "),
           planning.region = str_replace(planning.region, " Minnesota", ""),
           planning.region = fct_relevel(planning.region, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southeast"),
           edr = fct_relevel(edr, "EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "EDR 4 - West Central", "EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central", "EDR 8 - Southwest", "EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities", "Minnesota"))

counties.regions <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Join docs/county_regions.csv") %>%
  rename(mif = `MIF Region`) %>%
  mutate(countyfp = formatC(countyfp, width = 3, flag = "0"),
         Name = str_to_title(Name),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Q", "q"),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Of The", "of the"),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Mcleod", "McLeod"),
         Dem_Desc = ifelse(Name == "Minnesota", "Minnesota", Dem_Desc) ,
         edr = str_replace(edr, "  ", " "),
         planning.region = str_replace(planning.region, " Minnesota", ""),
         planning.region = fct_relevel(planning.region, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southeast"),
         edr = fct_relevel(edr, "EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "EDR 4 - West Central", "EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central", "EDR 8 - Southwest", "EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities", "Minnesota"),
         Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Entirely rural", "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban"))

counties.regions.1 <- counties.regions %>%
  mutate(statefp = "27",
         project.pr = ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 4 - West Central"), "Northwest",
                             ifelse(edr == "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "Northeast",
                                    ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central"), "Central",
                                           ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 8 - Southwest"), "Southwest",
                                                  ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast"), "Southern", as.character(planning.region)))))),
         project.pr = fct_relevel(project.pr, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southern"))


color.ruca <- c("Entirely rural" = "#009933", "Town/rural mix" = "#99CC33", "Urban/town/rural mix" = "#CC9966", "Entirely urban" = "#754C29", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.pr <- c("Northwest" = 	"#4575b4", "Northeast" = "grey", "Central" = "#fee090", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul" = "#d73027", "Southwest" = "#91bfdb", "Southeast" = "#fc8d59", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.edr <- c("EDR 1 - Northwest" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2 - Headwaters" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4 - West Central" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5 - North Central" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E- East Central" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W- Central" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8 - Southwest" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9 - South Central" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10 - Southeast" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities" = "#d8b365", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.edr.simple <- c("EDR 1" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11" = "#d8b365", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.pr.edr <- c ("Northwest" = "#4575b4","Northeast" = "#e0f3f8", "Central" = "#fee090", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul" = "#d73027", "Southwest" = "#91bfdb", "Southeast" = "#fc8d59", "Minnesota" = "black", "EDR 1 - Northwest" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2 - Headwaters" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4 - West Central" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5 - North Central" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E- East Central" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W- Central" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8 - Southwest" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9 - South Central" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10 - Southeast" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities" = "#d8b365")

mn_counties <- st_read("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Shapefiles/County shapefiles/MNCounties_MNDOT.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
  ms_simplify(keep = .01, keep_shapes = TRUE) %>%
  rename(countyfp = FIPS_CODE)
```

```{r master}
master.original <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-after-ct.csv") 

kable(names(master.original))

kable(head(master.original))
```

# Research Questions: Workforce Trajectories of Non-College Graduates in Southwest Minnesota

This phase of the analysis examines workforce participation trajectories among high school graduates in Southwest Minnesota who did not complete a postsecondary credential. Rather than relying on a single point-in-time workforce outcome, the analysis focuses on patterns of labor market attachment during the first five years after exiting formal education, with particular attention to persistence, instability, and regional attachment.

<br>

# Study Population and Group Definitions

**Study Population**

The analytic population consists of individuals who:

- Graduated from a high school in Southwest Minnesota,
- Did not complete a postsecondary credential (`ps.grad = No`), and
- there first five years after exiting formal education (high school or attending post-secondary) does not go beyond 2023.

<br>

**Definition of Workforce Disconnection**

Workforce participation is measured annually during the first five years after high school or existing post-secondary attendance. Individuals are classified as **disconnected** in a given year if they are categorized as either: - Not meaningful workforce participation, or - No Minnesota employment record.

Using this annual classification, individuals are grouped based on the persistence of disconnection during years 1 through 5:

- **Disconnected (Broad Definition):**\
  Disconnected in three or more of the first five years after graduation.

- **Persistently Disconnected:**\
  Disconnected in four or five of the first five years after graduation.

- **Intermittently Disconnected:**\
  Disconnected in one or two of the first five years after graduation.

These definitions emphasize sustained labor market detachment rather than short-term transitions.

<br>

**Regional Attachment**

For years in which individuals are employed but not meaningfully attached to the workforce, employment location is classified as:

- Within Southwest Minnesota, or
- Outside Southwest Minnesota (elsewhere in Minnesota).

This distinction is used to assess whether workforce disconnection reflects regional underutilization or disengagement from the regional labor market.

<br>

**Framing Consideration**

Together, these definitions allow the analysis to move beyond point-in-time workforce outcomes and toward a trajectory-based understanding of early-career labor market experiences. This approach supports clearer interpretation of workforce instability, recovery, and regional retention among non-college graduates in Southwest Minnesota.

<br>

# Methodology and Analytic Steps

This analysis examines workforce participation trajectories among non-college graduates in Southwest Minnesota, with a focus on identifying patterns of workforce disconnection, regional attachment, and employment stability during the first five years after high school.

## Step 1: Data Preparation and Analytic Population

**1.1 Analytic Population**

The analytic population is restricted to individuals who:

- Graduated from a high school in Southwest Minnesota,
- Did not complete a postsecondary credential (`ps.grad = No`), and
- Have complete workforce participation categorizations ("Sustained WF - Region", "Sustained WF - MN", "No MN employment record" or "Non-sustained WF") for the first five years after graduating high school or first five years after exiting post-secondary (but never completing).

**1.2 Annual Workforce Participation Classification**

For each individual, annual workforce participation status is identified for: - `grad.year.1` - `grad.year.2` - `grad.year.3` - `grad.year.4` - `grad.year.5`

It's important to note here that the term "grad.year.x" will be switched to mean years after exiting formal education, not just exiting high school.

Each year is classified into one of the following categories:

- Sustained WF participation (Sustained WF - Region or Sustained WF - MN)
- Non-sustained WF
- No Minnesota employment record.

**1.3 Workforce Disconnection Indicators**

For each annual observation, a binary **disconnection indicator** is created:

- **Disconnected (year-level)** = Not meaningful workforce participation **or** No Minnesota employment record, vs
- **Not disconnected =** Sustained WF - Region or Sustained WF - MN

Using these annual indicators, individuals are grouped based on persistence of disconnection across years 1–5:

- **Persistently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 4 or 5 of the first 5 years
- **Disconnected (Broad):** Disconnected in 3 of the first 5 years
- **Intermittently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 1–2 of the first 5 years
- **Consistently Attached:** Disconnected in 0 of the first 5 years

These categories emphasize sustained labor market attachment or detachment rather than point-in-time outcomes.

**1.4 Regional Location of Not Meaningful Employment**

For years in which individuals are categorized as **not meaningfully attached to the workforce**, employment location is further classified as: - Within Southwest Minnesota - Outside Southwest Minnesota (elsewhere in Minnesota) - Both (simultaneous employment inside and outside the region)

This classification allows identification of regional underemployment versus regional disengagement.

**1.5 Employment Intensity and Job Holding**

For each individual and each year (years 1–5), the following employment intensity measures are constructed: - Number of distinct employers during the year - Indicator for multiple job holding (two or more employers)

These measures are used to distinguish underemployment from precarious or fragmented employment.

<br>

## Step 2: Descriptive and Summary Statistics

**2.1 Distribution of Workforce Disconnection**

Summary statistics are produced to describe:

- The proportion of non-college graduates in each workforce disconnection category
- Differences in persistence and intermittency of disconnection

This establishes the overall scope and severity of workforce disconnection in early adulthood.

**2.2 Regional Attachment and Job Structure**

Among individuals experiencing workforce disconnection, summary statistics examine:

- The location of not meaningful employment (within region, outside region, both)
- Average and median number of employers per year
- Prevalence of multiple job holding

These summaries clarify whether disconnection reflects local underemployment, out-of-region work, or fragmented employment patterns.

<br>

## Step 3: Relationship Between Individual Characteristics and Disconnection Trajectories

**3.1 Variables of Interest**

The analysis examines associations between workforce disconnection categories and key individual characteristics, including:

- Demographics
  - `Gender`
  - `EconomicStatus`
  - `SpecialEdStatus`
  - `RaceEthnicity`
  - `english.learner`
  - `non.english.home`
- High school characteristics
  - `Dem_Desc`
  - `edr`
  - `avg.unemp.rate`
  - `avg.wages.pct.state`
- High school academics
  - `pseo.participant`
  - `took.ACT`
  - `ACTCompositeScore`
  - `ap.exam`
  - `MCA.M`
  - `MCA.R`
  - `MCA.S`
  - `cte.achievement`
  - `total.cte.courses.taken`
- Post-secondary
  - `attended.ps`

**3.2 Analytic Approach**

- Categorical variables are examined using cross-tabulations and measures of association (e.g., Cramer’s V).
- Continuous variables are examined using ANOVA.
- Interpretation emphasizes substantive differences and patterns rather than statistical significance alone.

<br>

# Data prep

## Step 1: Analytic population

The first step is filtering for our population;

- `ps.grad` = "No"

```{r}
analytic.pop <- master.original %>%
  filter(ps.grad == "No",
         grad.year.5 != "After 2023") 

kable(head(analytic.pop))
kable(names(analytic.pop))
```

## Step 2: Identify Workforce State for 5 Years After Formal Education

Next, we need to pull out all the individuals in which we have five years of workforce categories after they either graduate from high school or the last year in which they exited post-secondary attendance.

After that, we will categorize their workforce participation state into the following;

- Sustained WF - Region
- Sustained WF - MN
- Non-sustained WF
- No MN employment record.

I already created the annual workforce participation states for every individuals one year after graduation through 15 years after graduation. I will bring that list in and filter it by our analytic population.

```{r}
annual.wf.states <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/States/annual-seven-states.csv") %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, states) %>%
  mutate(grad.year = fct_relevel(grad.year, "grad.year.0", "grad.year.1", "grad.year.2", "grad.year.3", "grad.year.4", "grad.year.5", "grad.year.6", "grad.year.7", "grad.year.8", "grad.year.9", "grad.year.10", "grad.year.11", "grad.year.12", "grad.year.13", "grad.year.14", "grad.year.15")) 

no_ps_attendance <- analytic.pop %>%
  filter(attended.ps == "No") %>%
  select(PersonID) %>%
  left_join(annual.wf.states, by = "PersonID") %>%
  filter(states != "After 2023",
         states != "Attending ps",
         grad.year %in% c("grad.year.1", "grad.year.2", "grad.year.3", "grad.year.4", "grad.year.5")) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  mutate(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(n == 5) %>%
  mutate(states = as_factor(states))

last_ps_attendance <- analytic.pop %>%
  filter(attended.ps == "Yes") %>%
  select(PersonID) %>%
  left_join(annual.wf.states, by = "PersonID") %>%
  filter(states != "After 2023") %>%
  mutate(numeric.grad.year = str_sub(grad.year, -2, -1),
         numeric.grad.year = str_replace(numeric.grad.year, "\\.", ""),
         numeric.grad.year = as.integer(numeric.grad.year)) %>%
  filter(states == "Attending ps") %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  summarize(last_ps_year = max(numeric.grad.year, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() 

ps_attendance <- annual.wf.states %>%
  right_join(last_ps_attendance, by = "PersonID") %>%
  filter(states != "After 2023") %>%
  mutate(numeric.grad.year = str_sub(grad.year, -2, -1),
         numeric.grad.year = str_replace(numeric.grad.year, "\\.", ""),
         numeric.grad.year = as.integer(numeric.grad.year),
         final.year = last_ps_year + 5) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  filter(numeric.grad.year <= final.year,
         numeric.grad.year > last_ps_year) %>%
  mutate(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  filter(n == 5) %>%
  select(PersonID, grad.year, states, n)

master.annual.wf.five.year.states <- ps_attendance %>%
  rbind(no_ps_attendance) %>%
  mutate(states = str_replace(states, "Not meaningful, not attending ps", "Non-sustained WF"),
         states = str_replace(states, "No MN emp record, not attending ps", "No MN employment record"),
         states = ifelse(states %in% c("Meaningful emp County", "Meaningful emp EDR", "Meaningful emp region"), "Sustained WF - Region", states),
         states = str_replace(states, "Meaningful emp MN", "Sustained WF - MN"),
         states = fct_relevel(states, "Sustained WF - Region", "Sustained WF - MN", "Non-sustained WF", "No MN employment record")) %>%
  select(-n) %>%
  group_by(PersonID) %>%
  arrange(grad.year) %>%
  mutate(year.after.exit = seq(n())) %>%
  ungroup()

kable(head(master.annual.wf.five.year.states))

kable(names(master.annual.wf.five.year.states))

```

We now have a dataset consisting of `r comma(nrow(master.annual.wf.five.year.states))` individuals that meet the criteria that we want to examine along with their workforce categories from 1 to 5 years after exiting formal education.

<br>

## Step 3: Workforce Disconnection Indicators

Next we add another category to determine the level of disconnection, if any, for each individual. Disconnected is defined as any individual that was categorized as not having meaningful workforce participation or no Minnesota employment record in a given year one through five years after exiting formal education.

The level of disconnection is defined as the following;

- **Persistently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 4 or 5 of the first 5 years
- **Broadly Disconnected:** Disconnected in 3 of the first 5 years
- **Intermittently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 1–2 of the first 5 years
- **Consistently Attached:** Disconnected in 0 of the first 5 years

```{r}
disconnected.level <- master.annual.wf.five.year.states %>%
  mutate(disconnected = ifelse(states %in% c("Non-sustained WF", "No MN employment record"), "Disconnected", "Attached"),
         disconnected = as.factor(disconnected)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID, disconnected) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  complete(PersonID, disconnected, fill = list(n = 0)) %>%
  filter(disconnected == "Disconnected") %>%
  mutate(disconnected.cat = ifelse(n == 0, "Consistently attached",
                                   ifelse(n %in% c(1,2), "Intermittently disconnected",
                                          ifelse(n == 3, "Broadly disconnected",
                                                 ifelse(n %in% c(4,5), "Persistently disconnected", "A")))),
         disconnected.cat = fct_relevel(disconnected.cat, "Consistently attached", "Intermittently disconnected", "Broadly disconnected", "Persistently disconnected"))

kable(names(disconnected.level))
kable(head(disconnected.level))
```

The dataset now has `r comma(nrow(disconnected.level))` individuals that did not graduate college AND have 5 years of workforce categories immediately after exiting formal education categorized by workforce disconnection levels.

## **Step 4 & 5: Regional Location of Not Meaningful Employment**

For years in which individuals are categorized as **not meaningfully attached to the workforce**, employment location is further classified as: - Within Southwest Minnesota - Outside Southwest Minnesota (elsewhere in Minnesota) - Both (simultaneous employment inside and outside the region)

This classification allows identification of regional underemployment versus regional disengagement.

The population of individuals we need are the following;

- Did not graduate college
- For each individual categorize whether they had not meaningful workforce participation in the region, not in the region, or both.

We will use the `master.annual.wf.five.year.states` dataset as the starting point. We will have to have the year they graduated high school so when I match up employment records I'm only using employment records for the first 5 years after exiting formal education.

```{r}
hs.grad.year <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/K12 Enrollment/K12Enrollment-master.csv")

analytic.pop.annual.wf.states.hs.grad.year <- master.annual.wf.five.year.states %>%
  left_join(hs.grad.year[,c(1,9)], by = "PersonID")  %>%
  rename(after.ed.year = grad.year.x,
         hs.grad.year = grad.year.y) %>%
  mutate(numeric.after.ed.year = str_sub(after.ed.year, -2, -1),
         numeric.after.ed.year = str_replace(numeric.after.ed.year, "\\.", ""),
         numeric.after.ed.year = as.integer(numeric.after.ed.year),
         emp.year = hs.grad.year + numeric.after.ed.year) 
  
kable(head(analytic.pop.annual.wf.states.hs.grad.year))

kable(names(analytic.pop.annual.wf.states.hs.grad.year))
```

Now we need to bring in the employment data for Southwest graduates and then filter for PersonID that are in the newly created dataset. The columns we are going to keep from the employment dataset are the following;

- PersonID: Identifies the graduate from Southwest Minnesota
- TimeID: Last day of fiscal quarter
- EmploymentOrganizationID: An internal identification for each employer.
- EmployeeQuarterlyHoursWorked: The total number of hours worked by an employee at an employer’s location during a specific calendar quarter
- EmployeeQuarterlyWages: The total wages earned by an employee at an employer’s location during a specific calendar quarter
- EmployeeTenure: The total number of quarters a worker has been employeed by an organization at a specific location, up to an including the current calendar quarter
- CountyName: The name of the county corresponding to the County and MNCounty Codes for this Location.

```{r}
employment.original <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Employment/Employment-Southwest.csv") %>%
  drop_na(PersonID)

emp.year <- employment.original %>%
  filter(!is.na(TimeID)) %>%
  mutate(emp.year = str_sub(TimeID, 1, 4),
         emp.year = as.integer(emp.year),
         quarter = str_sub(TimeID, 5,6),
         quarter = ifelse(quarter == "03", 1,
                          ifelse(quarter == "06", 2,
                                 ifelse(quarter == "09", 3,
                                        ifelse(quarter == "12", 4, 0))))) %>%
  select(PersonID, EmploymentOrganizationID, EmployeeQuarterlyHoursWorked, EmployeeTenure, CountyName, emp.year, quarter) %>%
  mutate(CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, " County", ""),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, " County", ""),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, "Saint Louis", "St. Louis"),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, "City of Duluth", "St. Louis"),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, "City of Minneapolis", "Hennepin"),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, "City of St. Paul", "Ramsey"),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, "Shakopee Mdewakanton Community", "Scott"),
         CountyName = str_replace(CountyName, "White Earth Tribal Counci", "Mahnomen")) %>%
  left_join(counties.regions.1[,c(1,2,10)], by = c("CountyName" = "Name"))

not.meaningful.pop.emp.year <- analytic.pop.annual.wf.states.hs.grad.year %>%
  filter(states == "Non-sustained WF") %>%
  select(PersonID, emp.year)

not.meaningful.emp.quarter <- not.meaningful.pop.emp.year %>%
  left_join(emp.year, by = c("PersonID", "emp.year")) %>%
  mutate(project.pr = ifelse(is.na(project.pr), "Unclassified", as.character(project.pr)),
         in_region = (project.pr == "Southwest"),
         unclassified_region = (project.pr == "Unclassified"),
         out_region = c(!in_region & !unclassified_region)) %>%
  group_by(PersonID, emp.year, quarter) %>%
  summarise(n.emp = n(),
         any_in_quarter = any(in_region, na.rm = TRUE),
         any_out_quarter = any(out_region, na.rm = TRUE),
         any_unclassified_quarter = any(unclassified_region, na.rm = TRUE),
         any_mixed_qtr =  (sum(c(any_in_quarter, any_out_quarter, any_unclassified_quarter), na.rm = TRUE) >= 2)) %>%
  ungroup()

not.meaningful.emp.annual <- not.meaningful.emp.quarter %>%
  group_by(PersonID, emp.year) %>%
  summarize(multiple_emp_same_quarter = any(n.emp > 1),
            max_emp_in_qtr = max(n.emp),
            any_in_region = any(any_in_quarter, na.rm = TRUE),
            any_out_region = any(any_out_quarter, na.rm = TRUE),
            any_unclassified_region = any(any_unclassified_quarter, na.rm = TRUE),
            any_mixed_region = any(any_mixed_qtr, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(region_location_year = case_when(any_in_region & !any_out_region & !any_unclassified_region ~ "in_only", !any_in_region & any_out_region & !any_unclassified_region ~ "out_only", !any_in_region & !any_out_region & any_unclassified_region  ~ "unclassified_only", TRUE ~ "mixed" )) %>%
  mutate(
    mixed_type = case_when(
      region_location_year != "mixed" ~ NA_character_,
      any_in_region & any_out_region & !any_unclassified_region ~ "in_out",
      any_in_region & any_unclassified_region & !any_out_region ~ "in_unclassified",
      any_out_region & any_unclassified_region & !any_in_region ~ "out_unclassified",
      any_in_region & any_out_region & any_unclassified_region  ~ "in_out_unclassified"
    ),
    both_structure = case_when(region_location_year != "mixed" ~ NA_character_,
                               any_mixed_region ~ "simultaneous",
                               !any_mixed_region ~ "sequential",
                               TRUE ~ NA_character_)) %>%
  select(-any_in_region, -any_out_region, -any_unclassified_region, - any_mixed_region)


kable(head(not.meaningful.emp.annual))

kable(names(not.meaningful.emp.annual))
```

We have created a dataset with `r comma(nrow(not.meaningful.emp.annual))` individuals that do not have meaningful workforce participation for any given year up to 5 years after graduating high school that also provides if they had multiple employers in one quarter, the max number of different employers in one quarter for that employment year, and whether the employer was located in the region, outside the region, unclassified, or a mixture of these categories. Another column further refines the "mixed" category into whether it was sequential - meaning the mix occurred in separate quarters - or simultaneous - meaning there was a mix within the same quarter.

Next, we will piece this back together with the entire analytic population and create a complete annual workforce state for all individuals that did not complete post-secondary and has 5 years of workforce categories after graduating high school.

```{r}
Master.not.meaningful <- not.meaningful.emp.annual %>%
  left_join(hs.grad.year[,c(1, 9)], by = "PersonID") %>%
  rename(hs.grad.year = grad.year) %>%
  mutate(grad.year = emp.year - hs.grad.year,
         grad.year = paste("grad.year.", grad.year, sep = "")) %>%
  complete(PersonID, grad.year) %>%
  mutate(grad.year = fct_relevel(grad.year, "grad.year.0", "grad.year.1", "grad.year.2", "grad.year.3", "grad.year.4", "grad.year.5", "grad.year.6", "grad.year.7", "grad.year.8", "grad.year.9", "grad.year.10", "grad.year.11", "grad.year.12", "grad.year.13", "grad.year.14", "grad.year.15"))


Master.annual.wf.states.disconnected <- master.annual.wf.five.year.states %>%
  left_join(Master.not.meaningful, by = c("PersonID", "grad.year")) %>%
  unite("wf.states.refined",
        states, region_location_year, mixed_type, both_structure,
        sep = "_",
        na.rm = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(year.after.exit = paste("after.ed.", year.after.exit, sep = "")) %>%
  select(PersonID, year.after.exit, wf.states.refined) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = year.after.exit, values_from = wf.states.refined) %>%
  left_join(disconnected.level[,c(1,4)], by = "PersonID")

kable(head(Master.annual.wf.states.disconnected)) 

kable(names(Master.annual.wf.states.disconnected))
```

Okay, now I need to bring this all together.

```{r}
master <- analytic.pop %>%
  right_join(Master.annual.wf.states.disconnected, by = "PersonID") %>%
  mutate(disconnected.cat = fct_relevel(disconnected.cat, "Consistently attached", "Intermittently disconnected", "Broadly disconnected", "Persistently disconnected"))

kable(head(master))

kable(names(master))
```

The data set has `r comma(nrow(master))` individuals in it. These individuals did not complete post-secondary and had workforce categories for 5 years after exiting formal education.

<br>

# Descriptive analysis

## **Distribution of Workforce Disconnection**

Summary statistics are produced to describe: - The proportion of non-college graduates in each workforce disconnection category - Differences in persistence and intermittency of disconnection

This establishes the overall scope and severity of workforce disconnection in early adulthood.

Let's first examine the disconnected categories among these individuals. The level of disconnection is defined as the following;

- **Persistently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 4 or 5 of the first 5 years
- **Disconnected (Broad):** Disconnected in 3 of the first 5 years
- **Intermittently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 1–2 of the first 5 years
- **Consistently Attached:** Disconnected in 0 of the first 5 years

The chart below shows why it's so important to understand this particular group.

- Over half of non-college grads are disconnected from the labor force for at least 4 of the first five years after exiting formal education.
- The second lowest percentage of non-college grads are consistently attached - 14%.

Overall, this distribution shows that early labor force disconnection among non-college graduates is **predominantly persistent rather than intermittent**, underscoring the importance of focusing analytic and policy attention on long-term structural barriers to stable employment rather than short-lived employment gaps.

```{r}
data <- master %>%
  group_by(disconnected.cat) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n),
         data_id = seq(n()),
         disconnected.cat = fct_relevel(disconnected.cat, "Consistently attached", "Intermittently disconnected", "Broadly disconnected",  "Persistently disconnected"))

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(disconnected.cat, pct, fill = disconnected.cat)) +
  geom_col_interactive(position = "dodge", aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(disconnected.cat, "\nN: ", comma(n), "\n%: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, color = "black", size = 5, position = position_dodge(width = .9)) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Percent share of non-college grads,\n2008 - 2018 high school grads")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_bar+
  scale_fill_manual(values = brewer.pal(n = 5, "RdYlBu"),
                    guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom",
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 8, height_svg = 8) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))      


```

<br>

## **Regional Attachment and Job Structure**

Among individuals experiencing workforce disconnection, summary statistics examine: - The location of not meaningful employment (within region, outside region, both) - Average and median number of employers per year - Prevalence of multiple job holding

These summaries clarify whether disconnection reflects local underemployment, out-of-region work, or fragmented employment patterns.

Let's start with the distribution of the various workforce states with the newly defined categories within "Not meaningful" but not all of them because it will get too messy. Let's first do the following categories;

1.  Attending ps
2.  Meaningful emp region
3.  Meaningful emp MN
4.  Not meaningful
    1.  In only
    2.  Out only
    3.  Unclassified only
    4.  Mixed
5.  No MN emp record

Several clear and consistent patterns emerge across the five post-exit years among non-college completers.

The no MN employment record category is the single largest workforce state at every time point, and it grows steadily throughout the observation window — from 28.8% in year 1 to 34.9% by year 5. This is the most striking feature of the chart: the most common experience for non-college completers in any given year is complete absence from Minnesota employment records, and that absence becomes more entrenched over time rather than resolving.

Sustained regional employment is the second most common state, holding remarkably stable across all five years — ranging narrowly from 22.3% to 26.5% with no clear directional trend. This stability suggests that those who attach to the regional labor market do so relatively quickly and maintain that attachment.

Sustained statewide employment outside the region shows modest growth over time, rising from 9.9% at year 1 to 15.6% by year 5, consistent with graduates gradually finding stable employment elsewhere in Minnesota.

Among the non-sustained workforce participation subcategories, in-region only non-sustained employment is the largest component and declines slightly over time (19.7% to 10.8%), suggesting some conversion into sustained regional employment as graduates gain experience. Out-of-region non-sustained employment remains small and stable (7.7%–7.8%). The unclassified and mixed categories are negligible throughout.

Taken together, the annual distributions are consistent with the trajectory findings: a stable core of regional workers, a growing group of statewide workers, a modest conversion from non-sustained to sustained regional employment over time, and a large and growing share that never establishes a Minnesota employment footprint across the entire five-year window.

```{r}
wf.states.factors <- c("Attending ps", "Sustained WF - Region", "Sustained WF - MN", "Non-sustained WF_in_only", "Non-sustained WF_out_only", "Non-sustained WF_unclassified_only", "Non-sustained WF - mixed", "No MN employment record")

data <- Master.annual.wf.states.disconnected %>%
  select(PersonID:after.ed.5) %>%
  pivot_longer(names_to = "grad.year", values_to = "wf.states", 2:ncol(.)) %>%
  mutate(wf.states = ifelse(wf.states %in% c("Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_sequential", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_unclassified_simultaneous" , "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_unclassified_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_out_unclassified_simultaneous", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_out_unclassified_sequential", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_unclassified_sequential", "Non-sustained WF_mixed_in_out_unclassified_sequential"), "Non-sustained WF - mixed", wf.states),
         wf.states = fct_relevel(wf.states, wf.states.factors)) %>%
  group_by(grad.year, wf.states) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(grad.year) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(data_id = seq(n()))


plot <- ggplot(data, aes(wf.states, pct, fill = wf.states)) +
  facet_wrap(~grad.year, ncol = 2) +
  geom_col_interactive(position = "dodge", aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(grad.year, "\nWorkforce category: ", wf.states, "\nN: ", comma(n), "\nPercent: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, color = "white", size = 5, position = position_dodge(width = .9)) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Share of non-college completers by workforce participation categories the first five years after exiting formal education")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_bar+
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#8c510a", "#bf812d", "#dfc27d", "#c7eae5", "#80cdc1", "#35978f", "#01665e", "black"),
                    guide = guide_legend(ncol = 3)) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 10, height_svg = 10) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))      

```

Let's dive a bit deeper into the disconnected group - not meaningful workforce participation and individuals with No MN employment. The chart below shows the percentage of disconnected individuals (not meaningful, no MN employment record) that fall into each of the refined categories.

This more granular view confirms and extends the patterns observed in the simplified chart, with a few additional insights emerging from the decomposition of non-sustained workforce participation.

The two dominant categories — no MN employment record and sustained regional employment — behave exactly as expected. No MN employment record grows steadily from 28.8% to 34.9%, while sustained regional employment grows modestly from 22.3% to 26.5% and then plateaus. These two categories together account for roughly half of all observations at every time point and move in opposite directions, suggesting a clean bifurcation between those who attach to the regional labor market and those who disengage from Minnesota employment entirely.

Sustained statewide employment grows consistently from 9.9% to 15.6%, the clearest upward trend in the data outside of no MN employment record. This reflects gradual out-of-region labor market settlement over the five years.

Among the non-sustained subcategories, in-region only non-sustained employment is the largest and shows the most meaningful trend — declining from 19.7% to 10.8% over five years. This decline, paired with the modest growth in sustained regional employment, is consistent with a conversion process in which workers who begin with fragmented regional employment gradually stabilize into sustained regional attachment. The roughly nine- percentage-point decline in in-region non-sustained corresponds reasonably well with the four-percentage- point gain in sustained regional employment over the same window, with some of the remainder presumably converting to no MN record or statewide employment.

Out-of-region non-sustained employment is small and stable throughout (7.8% declining slightly to 7.0%), suggesting this is a persistent state for a small subset rather than a transitional one.

The mixed and unclassified non-sustained subcategories are negligible in magnitude — all below 3% and most below 1% — and show no meaningful trends. Their collective contribution to the overall non-sustained picture is minimal, and collapsing them for analytical purposes appears well-justified.

One detail worth flagging: the attending ps row shows 0.0% across all five years, which confirms that the exclusion of attending ps observations from the analytic population was implemented correctly — no postsecondary attendance years are appearing in the post-exit trajectory window.

Overall, this decomposition supports a simplified three-category characterization for most analytical purposes: sustained regional employment, sustained or transitioning statewide employment, and no MN employment record — with in-region non-sustained as a meaningful fourth category given its size and directional trend toward regional attachment over time.

```{r}
refined.disconnected.factors <- c( "Not meaningful_in_only",  "Not meaningful_out_only", "Not meaningful_unclassified_only", "Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_simultaneous", "Not meaningful_mixed_in_unclassified_simultaneous", "Not meaningful_mixed_out_unclassified_simultaneous", "Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_unclassified_simultaneous","Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_sequential", "Not meaningful_mixed_in_unclassified_sequential", "Not meaningful_mixed_out_unclassified_sequential", "Not meaningful_mixed_in_out_unclassified_sequential", "No MN emp record")

data <- Master.annual.wf.states.disconnected %>%
  select(PersonID:after.ed.5) %>%
  pivot_longer(names_to = "grad.year", values_to = "wf.state", 2:ncol(.)) %>%
  filter(!wf.state %in% c("Meaningful emp region", "Meaningful emp MN", "Attending ps")) %>%
  mutate(wf.state = fct_relevel(wf.state, refined.disconnected.factors)) %>%
  group_by(grad.year, wf.state) %>%
  summarize(n = n()) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  group_by(grad.year) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n)) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  mutate(data_id = seq(n()))

plot <- ggplot(data, aes(grad.year, pct, fill = wf.state)) +
  facet_wrap(~wf.state, ncol = 2) +
  geom_col_interactive(position = "dodge", aes(data_id = data_id, tooltip = paste(grad.year, "\nWorkforce category: ", wf.state, "\nN: ", comma(n), "\nPercent: ", percent(pct, accuracy = .1), sep = ""))) +
  geom_label(aes(label = percent(pct, accuracy = .1)), show.legend = FALSE, color = "white", size = 5, position = position_dodge(width = .9)) +
  labs(x="", y = "", color="", title = "Percent share of disconnected workforce states")+
  scale_y_continuous(labels=scales::percent)+
  theme_bar+
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        text = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 25, hjust = .9))


girafe(ggobj = plot, width_svg = 8, height_svg = 12) %>%
  girafe_options(opts_selection(type = "none"),
                 opts_sizing(rescale = FALSE))      
```

<br>

## What does this tell us?

The early post-exit labor market experience of non-college completers in Southwest Minnesota is defined by two competing gravitational pulls — one toward the regional economy, one toward complete disengagement — with a relatively small middle ground in between.

About a quarter of non-college completers attach to the regional labor market quickly and stay there. Their employment stabilizes within the first year or two and remains anchored to Southwest Minnesota across the entire five-year window. For this group, the transition out of formal education into steady regional work is relatively clean.

A second group — growing steadily from about 10% to 16% over five years — finds its footing in employment elsewhere in Minnesota. Some of these individuals start in fragmented out-of-region work and gradually stabilize, others move more directly. By year 5 they represent a meaningful share of the population, but they are lost to the regional economy.

The largest and most troubling story belongs to the group with no Minnesota employment record, which starts at nearly 29% in year 1 and grows to 35% by year 5. This is not a temporary condition resolving itself over time — it is getting worse. A substantial and expanding share of non-college completers are simply not showing up in formal Minnesota employment at any point, and each passing year makes that absence more entrenched rather than less.

The remaining quarter or so are in non-sustained employment — working, but not consistently enough with a single employer to cross the meaningful employment threshold. The most important thing about this group is what happens within it over time: those working inside the region gradually convert toward sustained regional employment, while those working outside it largely stay stuck in fragmentation. In-region non- sustained employment drops by nearly nine percentage points across five years, feeding — at least in part — into the modest growth of sustained regional employment.

The overall picture is one of rapid and largely permanent sorting. Within the first year after exiting formal education, the broad contours of where someone will end up are already visible. The regional economy captures about a quarter, the broader Minnesota economy captures a growing share, and the largest single group drifts away from formal employment entirely — and keeps drifting.

<br>

# Relationship Between Individual Characteristics and the broad 5-year aggregate disconnection categories

The next section focuses on exploring what relationships exist between individual characteristics and the different ways in which disconnection from the workforce is measured using the larger non-refined categories;

- **Persistently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 4 or 5 of the first 5 years
- **Disconnected (Broad):** Disconnected in 3 of the first 5 years
- **Intermittently Disconnected:** Disconnected in 1–2 of the first 5 years
- **Consistently Attached:** Disconnected in 0 of the first 5 years

The variables that we will be including in this analysis are the following;

- Demographics
  - `Gender`
  - `EconomicStatus`
  - `SpecialEdStatus`
  - `RaceEthnicity`
  - `english.learner`
  - `non.english.home`
- High school characteristics
  - `Dem_Desc`
  - `edr`
  - `avg.unemp.rate`
  - `avg.wages.pct.state`
- High school academics
  - `pseo.participant`
  - `took.ACT`
  - `ACTCompositeScore`
  - `ap.exam`
  - `MCA.M`
  - `MCA.R`
  - `MCA.S`
  - `cte.achievement`
  - `total.cte.courses.taken`

Post-secondary

- `attended.ps`

For categorical variables analysis will be conducted using Cramer's V-test and continuous variables will be tested using ANOVA.

<br>

## Demographic variables

We are exploring the relationships between the following demographic variables and disconnection levels;

- `Gender`
- `EconomicStatus`
- `SpecialEdStatus`
- `RaceEthnicity`
- `english.learner`
- `non.english.home`

### Summary

Across the six demographic variables examined, associations with five-year disconnection trajectories are statistically significant but uniformly weak to modest in effect size, ranging from V=0.047 for economic status to V=0.109 for gender. This is consistent with the point-in-time bivariate findings, where demographic characteristics were generally weaker predictors than high school accomplishment variables.

The most consistent finding across all demographic variables is that persistent disconnection — the most severe trajectory outcome — is the primary site of differentiation. Rather than producing uniform shifts across all four disconnection categories, demographic disadvantage tends to concentrate individuals at the persistent end of the spectrum while leaving the middle categories (broad and intermittent disconnection) relatively undifferentiated. Consistent attachment is correspondingly depressed among disadvantaged groups, but the gap is generally smaller than the persistent disconnection gap.

Ranking the variables by effect size, gender (V=0.109) is the strongest demographic predictor of trajectory outcomes, with females showing a persistent disconnection rate more than eight percentage points above males and a consistent attachment rate six points below. Special education status (V=0.075) ranks second, with a similarly sized persistent disconnection gap and the added concern that special education graduates show lower intermittent disconnection as well — suggesting they are not merely shifting within the disconnection spectrum but are genuinely more likely to experience the most severe form of labor market detachment. English learner status (V=0.067) and non-English home language (V=0.066) rank third and fourth and are effectively interchangeable in their findings, both showing persistent disconnection rates approaching 59% — among the highest of any demographic subgroup examined. Race/ethnicity (V=0.053) ranks fifth overall but contains the most extreme subgroup finding in the entire demographic analysis: American Indian graduates show a persistent disconnection rate of 70.6%, a result that stands apart from all other groups regardless of the variable's modest overall effect size. Economic status (V=0.047) is the weakest demographic predictor, with differences across groups small enough to raise questions about its independent contribution once other variables are accounted for in multivariate models.

The overarching demographic story is one of compounding disadvantage concentrated at the severe end of the trajectory spectrum. Among non-college completers — already a population with limited formal credentials — being female, a special education participant, an English learner, or American Indian is associated with a meaningfully higher likelihood of spending most of the first five post-exit years disconnected from formal employment. These are not marginal differences. For several subgroups, persistent disconnection is not the worst-case outcome but the modal one.

<br>

::: panel-tabset
### `Gender`

Gender shows a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.109), with a moderate effect size that is somewhat stronger than the gender associations observed in the point-in-time workforce participation analysis at years 1 and 5, reflecting the added power of aggregating outcomes across the full five-year window.

The most striking difference is in persistent disconnection, where females show a substantially higher rate than males (55.4% vs. 46.8%) — a gap of more than eight percentage points. Conversely, males are considerably more likely to be consistently attached (16.6% vs. 10.2%), a six- percentage-point advantage that mirrors the sustained regional employment gap observed in the point-in-time analysis. Intermittent disconnection is modestly higher among males (25.6% vs. 22.5%), while broad disconnection is similar across genders (10.9% vs. 11.8%).

The overall pattern is clear: among non-college completers, females are substantially more likely to experience persistent labor market detachment across the first five years after exiting formal education, while males are more likely to establish consistent labor market attachment. This gender gap in trajectory outcomes is more pronounced than the point-in-time findings suggested, indicating that the cumulative burden of disconnection falls disproportionately on women in this population.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$Gender, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `economic.status`

Economic status shows a statistically significant but weak association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.047), notably weaker than the gender association and considerably weaker than the economic status associations observed in the point-in-time analysis at year 1 (V=0.137). This attenuation makes sense — the strong year 1 association was largely driven by differential postsecondary attendance, a distinction that is absorbed into the trajectory construction here and no longer differentiates the two groups in the same way.

The differences across economic status groups are small across all four disconnection categories. Economically disadvantaged graduates show modestly higher persistent disconnection (51.9% vs. 48.8%) and slightly lower consistent attachment (12.5% vs. 15.6%). Intermittent disconnection (24.4% vs. 24.3%) and broad disconnection (11.3% vs. 11.3%) are virtually identical across groups.

The practical takeaway is that economic disadvantage, while associated with somewhat worse trajectory outcomes, is not a strong differentiator of five-year disconnection patterns among non-college completers once the population is restricted to those who did not complete a credential. The three-percentage-point gap in persistent disconnection and the three-percentage-point gap in consistent attachment are real but modest, suggesting that other characteristics — particularly high school accomplishments — are likely to carry substantially more explanatory weight in distinguishing trajectory outcomes within this population.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$economic.status, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `SpecialEdStatus`

Special education status shows a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.075), stronger than economic status but weaker than gender. The pattern is consistent with the point-in-time findings, where special education status was the strongest demographic predictor — though the effect size here is considerably smaller than the V=0.171 observed at year 1, again reflecting the absorption of postsecondary attendance differences into the trajectory construction.

The most pronounced difference is in persistent disconnection, where special education graduates show a rate of 56.7% compared to 48.4% among non-special education graduates — a gap of more than eight percentage points that mirrors the gender gap in magnitude. Consistent attachment is notably lower among special education graduates (11.2% vs. 14.9%), and intermittent disconnection is also lower (21.2% vs. 25.3%), suggesting that special education graduates are not simply shifting between disconnection categories but are disproportionately concentrated at the most severe end of the trajectory spectrum.

The broad disconnection category shows little difference across groups (10.9% vs. 11.4%), indicating that the key divide is between persistent disconnection and the remaining categories rather than a uniform shift across the full distribution.

Overall, special education status is associated with a meaningfully higher likelihood of persistent labor market detachment across the first five years after exiting formal education. Combined with the point-in-time findings showing persistent employment instability and reduced statewide mobility, the trajectory analysis reinforces that special education graduates face compounding labor market disadvantages that are not resolved within the first five years after high school.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$SpecialEdStatus, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `RaceEthnicity`

Race/ethnicity shows a statistically significant but weak association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.053), consistent with the weak associations observed in the point-in-time analysis. Despite the small overall effect size, the variation across racial and ethnic groups is substantively meaningful, particularly at the extremes of the disconnection spectrum.

Persistent disconnection shows the most striking variation across groups. American Indian graduates show by far the highest persistent disconnection rate (70.6%), more than 20 percentage points above the overall rate of 50.4% and roughly 22 points above White graduates (48.6%). Black graduates (61.6%), Hispanic graduates (56.0%), and Asian/Pacific Islander graduates (56.5%) all show elevated persistent disconnection relative to White graduates, though the gaps are smaller than for American Indian graduates.

Consistent attachment follows the mirror-image pattern. White graduates show the highest consistent attachment rate (15.0%), while American Indian graduates show the lowest by a substantial margin (3.8%) — less than one-quarter the White rate. Asian/Pacific Islander graduates (11.6%) and Hispanic graduates (10.6%) fall below the White rate, while Black graduates show the lowest consistent attachment among non-American-Indian groups (7.2%).

Intermittent and broad disconnection show more modest variation across groups, with American Indian graduates again showing the most adverse profile — the lowest intermittent disconnection rate (15.3%) reflects not a favorable outcome but rather their concentration in the persistent disconnection category, leaving fewer individuals in the less severe categories.

The small group sizes for American Indian (n=235), Black (n=307), and Asian/Pacific Islander (n=490) graduates warrant caution in interpretation, but the directional patterns are consistent and the American Indian finding in particular — 70.6% persistently disconnected across the first five years after exiting formal education — is a sobering result regardless of sample size considerations.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$RaceEthnicity, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `english.learner`

English learner status shows a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.067), modest in magnitude but consistent in direction with the point-in-time findings. The pattern reinforces the distinctive labor market profile of English learners identified earlier — elevated absence from Minnesota employment records and reduced labor market attachment — now expressed as a trajectory disadvantage accumulated across five years.

Persistent disconnection is substantially higher among English learners (59.0% vs. 49.1%), a gap of nearly ten percentage points that is the largest demographic gap observed in this category aside from American Indian graduates in the race/ethnicity analysis. Consistent attachment is meaningfully lower among English learners (10.9% vs. 14.4%), and intermittent disconnection is also lower (21.2% vs. 24.8%), again indicating that English learners are not simply redistributed across disconnection categories but are disproportionately concentrated in persistent disconnection. Broad disconnection is modestly lower among English learners (8.9% vs. 11.7%), consistent with the same pattern of concentration at the severe end of the spectrum rather than in the middle categories.

The overall profile of English learners in the trajectory analysis is notably adverse: nearly three in five experience persistent labor market detachment across their first five post-exit years, while fewer than one in nine achieves consistent attachment. This cumulative disadvantage — expressed here as a trajectory rather than a point-in-time outcome — adds further weight to the concern raised in the bivariate analysis about the growing share of English learners absent from Minnesota employment records over time.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$english.learner, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `non.english.home`

Non-English home language shows a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.066), nearly identical in magnitude to English learner status (V=0.067) and consistent with the pattern observed throughout the bivariate analysis where these two variables produced closely parallel findings. As noted previously, the substantial overlap between English learner status and non-English home language makes their independent contributions difficult to disentangle in bivariate analysis, and multivariate modeling will be needed to assess which carries more unique explanatory weight.

The distributional pattern mirrors the English learner findings with high fidelity. Persistent disconnection is substantially elevated among graduates from non-English speaking homes (58.7% vs. 49.2%), a gap of nearly ten percentage points almost identical to the English learner gap. Consistent attachment is lower (10.1% vs. 14.5%), intermittent disconnection is modestly lower (21.9% vs. 24.7%), and broad disconnection is slightly lower (9.3% vs. 11.6%) — the same pattern of concentration at the persistent end of the spectrum rather than redistribution across middle categories.

The close correspondence between English learner and non-English home language findings across both the point-in-time and trajectory analyses suggests these variables are capturing largely the same underlying dynamic. Given that English learner status produced a marginally stronger effect size in the point-in-time analysis and a nearly identical one here, it remains the more precise of the two variables for characterizing this population's labor market disadvantage, though the substantive story told by either variable is effectively the same.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$non.english.home, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## High school characteristics

Here are the independent variables we are exploring;

- `Dem_Desc`
- `edr`
- `avg.unemp.rate`
- `avg.wages.pct.state`

### Summary

Among the four high school characteristics variables examined, the overall picture is one of weak to negligible associations with five-year disconnection trajectories, consistent with findings from both the point-in-time bivariate analysis and the full population analyses where these variables showed limited predictive value.

Community demographic description (V=0.025) produces the weakest association of any variable examined in either the demographic or high school characteristics groups — effectively zero in practical terms. Differences across entirely rural, town/rural mix, and urban/town/rural mix communities are negligible across all four disconnection categories, and this variable can be confidently excluded from multivariate modeling of trajectory outcomes.

Average county wages as a percent of the state average produces no statistically significant association whatsoever, with every pairwise Tukey comparison non-significant and absolute differences between disconnection categories approaching zero. This is the clearest null finding in the entire trajectory analysis and definitively closes the case for excluding this variable from further modeling.

Economic development region (V=0.053) and average county unemployment rate produce modest but interpretable associations. EDR shows a consistent gradient with EDR 6E graduates experiencing the lowest persistent disconnection (45.8%) and the highest consistent attachment (15.4%), reflecting the relatively stronger labor market environment of the Southwest Central region. County unemployment rate produces a counterintuitive pattern in which persistently disconnected graduates come from lower-unemployment counties than intermittently or broadly disconnected graduates — a finding consistent with the point-in-time interpretation that persistent disconnection is driven largely by absence from Minnesota employment records rather than local labor market failure, pointing toward out-of-state residence or informal employment rather than local joblessness.

Taken together, the high school characteristics variables contribute little to explaining five-year disconnection trajectory outcomes among non-college completers. The geographic and labor market context in which a student attended high school matters far less than what they did while they were there. This conclusion sets up the high school accomplishments variables as the expected primary source of explanatory power in the trajectory analysis, consistent with their stronger performance in the point-in-time bivariate analysis.

<br>

::: panel-tabset
### `Dem_Desc`

Community demographic description shows a statistically significant but negligible association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.025), the weakest effect size observed in either the point-in-time or trajectory analyses for any variable examined. While the association clears the threshold of statistical significance, it carries no practical weight as a predictor of disconnection trajectory outcomes.

Differences across the three community types are minimal. Entirely rural graduates show a modestly higher persistent disconnection rate (54.5%) relative to town/rural mix (49.7%) and urban/town/rural mix (50.5%), and a slightly lower consistent attachment rate (11.8% vs. 14.3% and 14.2%). All other category differences are negligible, with broad and intermittent disconnection rates nearly identical across all three community types.

This finding is consistent with the point-in-time analysis, where community demographic description showed weak associations that were deemed insufficient for inclusion in multivariate models. The trajectory analysis confirms that conclusion — where a graduate went to high school, in terms of community type, tells us very little about whether they will experience persistent, intermittent, or consistent labor market attachment across their first five post-exit years. This variable can be confidently set aside in subsequent multivariate modeling of disconnection trajectories.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$Dem_Desc, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `edr`

Economic development region shows a statistically significant but weak association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.053), consistent with the modest effect sizes observed in the point-in-time analysis. The pattern across the three EDRs is interpretable and aligns with the geographic labor market differences identified earlier, with EDR 6E showing the most favorable trajectory profile and EDR 8 the least.

Persistent disconnection shows the clearest gradient across EDRs: EDR 6E graduates show the lowest rate (45.8%), followed by EDR 6W (51.9%) and EDR 8 (53.5%). The gap between EDR 6E and EDR 8 is nearly eight percentage points — modest but consistent with the point-in-time finding that EDR 6E graduates show greater statewide labor market mobility and lower no MN employment record rates. Consistent attachment follows the same ordering, with EDR 6E highest (15.4%), EDR 8 next (13.4%), and EDR 6W lowest (12.6%), though the differences are small.

The most notable EDR 6E advantage is in intermittent disconnection (27.2% vs. 24.1% and 22.0%), suggesting that EDR 6E graduates are more likely to experience partial rather than complete labor market engagement during their transitional years — a profile associated with eventual attachment rather than prolonged detachment.

Overall, EDR adds modest but meaningful geographic context to the trajectory findings. Graduates from the Southwest Central region consistently show somewhat better disconnection trajectory outcomes than those from the Upper Minnesota Valley or Southwest EDRs, likely reflecting the stronger and more diverse local labor market centered around Marshall. However, effect sizes remain weak and EDR's independent contribution in multivariate models is expected to be limited given its correlation with other predictors.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master$disconnected.cat, var.col = master$edr, 
         title = "", 
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         use.viewer = TRUE)
```

### `avg.unemp.rate`

Average county unemployment rate shows a statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories, though the pattern is somewhat different from what might be expected and warrants careful interpretation.

The Tukey comparisons reveal that persistently disconnected graduates come from counties with meaningfully lower average unemployment rates than both intermittently disconnected (−0.17 percentage points, highly significant) and broadly disconnected graduates (−0.16 points, significant). Consistently attached graduates sit between these groups, not significantly different from persistently disconnected graduates (−0.08 points, p=0.107) but marginally different from intermittently disconnected graduates (0.10 points, p=0.046).

At first glance this seems counterintuitive — persistently disconnected graduates coming from lower-unemployment counties might suggest that local labor market conditions are not driving disconnection. However, this finding is consistent with the point- in-time analysis, which identified that the no MN employment record group — the primary driver of persistent disconnection — showed unemployment rate profiles similar to or below sustained regional workers. The interpretation offered there applies here as well: persistently disconnected graduates in lower-unemployment counties may not be failing to find local work so much as they are absent from Minnesota employment records entirely, possibly reflecting out-of-state residence, informal employment, or communities with labor market structures not fully captured by county unemployment rates.

Intermittently and broadly disconnected graduates — those who engage with the labor market inconsistently rather than persistently — come from the highest- unemployment counties, suggesting that local labor market tightness may contribute to the fragmented employment patterns characteristic of these middle trajectory categories.

Overall, county unemployment rate adds modest contextual nuance to the trajectory findings but does not produce a simple or intuitive gradient across disconnection categories, reinforcing the earlier conclusion that it is unlikely to contribute substantially in multivariate models.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ disconnected.cat, data = master)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
```

### `avg.wages.pct.state`

Average county wages as a percent of the state average shows no statistically significant association with five-year disconnection trajectories. Every pairwise comparison in the Tukey test is non-significant, with p-values ranging from 0.36 to 0.99, and the differences between disconnection categories are negligible in absolute terms — all contrasts fall within 0.003 percentage points of the state average, which is essentially zero for practical purposes.

This finding is consistent with and extends the point-in-time analysis, where county wage levels showed very small absolute differences across workforce participation categories and was identified as unlikely to contribute meaningful explanatory value in multivariate models. The trajectory analysis confirms that conclusion definitively — where a graduate's high school county ranks in terms of wages relative to the state average tells us nothing about whether they will experience persistent, intermittent, or consistent labor market attachment across their first five post-exit years.

This variable can be set aside entirely in subsequent multivariate modeling of disconnection trajectories.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ disconnected.cat, data = master)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)
```
:::

<br>

## High school academics

Here are the independent variables we are exploring;

- `pseo.participant`
- `took.ACT`
- `ACTCompositeScore`
- `ap.exam`
- `MCA.M`
- `MCA.R`
- `MCA.S`
- `cte.achievement`
- `total.cte.courses.taken`

### Summary

The high school accomplishments variables produce the strongest and most consistent associations with five-year disconnection trajectories of any variable group examined, confirming that what students did in high school is the primary differentiator of early labor market trajectory outcomes among non-college completers. Effect sizes are meaningfully larger than those observed in the demographic and high school characteristics groups, and the findings divide cleanly along the two-pathway structure identified throughout the broader analysis.

**CTE achievement** (V=0.106) is the strongest categorical predictor in the group and the strongest overall variable in the trajectory analysis. Its gradient across the three CTE categories is monotonic, large, and practically significant: CTE concentrators show a persistent disconnection rate of 44.8% compared to 63.3% among graduates with no CTE engagement — an 18-percentage-point gap that is the largest category-level difference observed for any variable in the entire trajectory analysis. The no CTE group's consistent attachment rate of 8.6% — less than half that of CTE concentrators — underscores that the absence of CTE engagement is the single strongest marker of trajectory disadvantage in this population.

**Total CTE courses taken** reinforces this conclusion from the continuous variable perspective, producing the only fully significant Tukey comparison set in the trajectory analysis — every pairwise contrast between all four disconnection categories is statistically significant. The clean four-tier ordering, with consistently attached graduates showing the highest mean CTE course-taking and persistently disconnected graduates the lowest (1.52 courses below), has a dose-response quality suggesting that each additional CTE course is associated with movement toward better trajectory outcomes. Together, CTE achievement and total CTE courses taken form the most powerful and coherent predictor cluster in the entire trajectory analysis.

**ACT participation** (V=0.075) is the strongest academic orientation predictor, showing a seven- percentage-point persistent disconnection advantage for ACT takers relative to non-takers and higher consistent attachment (15.9% vs. 12.6%). Its effect size is comparable to special education status in the demographic group, indicating that ACT participation retains meaningful predictive value for trajectory outcomes even after postsecondary attendance differences are absorbed into the population construction.

**MCA Math score** produces a clear two-tier structure in which consistently attached graduates score significantly above all three disconnection categories, with the three disconnection groups clustered together below. This straightforward positive association — higher math achievement linked to better trajectory outcomes — contrasts with the counterintuitive ACT Composite Score finding and reflects the broader population base of MCA Math, which is not subject to the out-of-state departure dynamic that affects ACT score interpretation.

**ACT Composite Score** produces the most analytically complex finding in the group: higher scores among ACT takers are paradoxically associated with persistent disconnection rather than consistent attachment. Persistently disconnected ACT takers score 0.68 points above consistently attached and 0.82 points above intermittently disconnected graduates. This finding is best interpreted not as higher-achieving graduates failing in the labor market but as higher- achieving non-completers leaving Minnesota — their persistent disconnection reflecting absence from Minnesota employment records rather than genuine labor market detachment. This interpretation requires explicit acknowledgment in multivariate modeling.

**MCA Reading score** shows the simplest and weakest pattern among the academic achievement measures — only the contrast between consistently attached and persistently disconnected graduates reaches significance, with all other pairwise comparisons non-significant. This is a notable reversal from the point-in-time analysis where MCA Reading was the strongest academic achievement predictor, suggesting that in the trajectory context math achievement is more directly relevant to the types of employment that produce consistent labor market attachment among non-college completers in this region.

**PSEO participation** (V=0.038) and **AP exam participation** (V=0.012, p=0.584) show negligible to null associations with disconnection trajectories. Both variables derived their point-in-time strength almost entirely from postsecondary attendance gaps that are structurally absorbed in the trajectory construction. Neither variable adds meaningful information about trajectory outcomes once the population is restricted to non-completers with complete five-year windows, and both can be confidently excluded from multivariate modeling.

**MCA Science** produces a null finding across all pairwise comparisons, consistent with its voluntary and self-selected nature and the largest missingness of any continuous variable in the analysis. It should be excluded from multivariate models.

The overarching conclusion from the high school accomplishments group is unambiguous: CTE engagement is the most powerful predictor of avoiding persistent labor market detachment among non-college completers, and its effect is large enough to dwarf every other variable examined in the trajectory analysis. Academic orientation variables — ACT participation, MCA Math — show meaningful but secondary associations. The variables that drove the strongest point-in-time associations through postsecondary attendance differentials (PSEO, AP exams) largely disappear once that structural advantage is removed. For multivariate modeling, the priority variables from this group are CTE achievement or total CTE courses taken, ACT participation, and MCA Math, with careful attention to the paradoxical ACT Composite Score finding.

<br>

::: panel-tabset
### `pseo.participant`

PSEO participation shows a statistically significant but negligible association with five-year disconnection trajectories (V=0.038), the weakest effect size among the high school accomplishments variables examined thus far and notably weaker than the point-in-time associations observed at year 1 (V=0.186). This attenuation is expected — the strong year 1 association was driven almost entirely by the postsecondary attendance gap, a distinction that is handled structurally in the trajectory construction and no longer differentiates the two groups here.

Differences across PSEO participation categories are small across all four disconnection outcomes. PSEO participants show modestly lower persistent disconnection (47.3% vs. 51.2%) and slightly higher consistent attachment (16.8% vs. 14.0%), while intermittent disconnection is nearly identical (24.1% vs. 24.3%) and broad disconnection shows a small difference (11.9% vs. 10.6%) that runs counter to the overall direction.

It is worth noting that this analysis is restricted to 2011 cohorts and later where PSEO data are available, reducing the sample to 8,951 individuals compared to 13,177 in the full trajectory population.
